title: All-butter shortbread rounds
subtitle: Scottish shortbread, plain and correct
excerpt: Scottish shortbread made with plain flour, icing sugar, and a generous amount of butter. Mixed by hand, rested in the fridge, cut into rounds and baked low and slow until pale gold. Makes sixteen biscuits.
sourceNotes: Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): shortbread documented as a Scottish household standard baked for Hogmanay and everyday use. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: shortbread listed among Scottish national biscuits. The high butter-to-flour ratio and icing sugar finish are the classical form recorded in both sources.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and fully cooled. Thaw at room temperature for 30 minutes. No reheating needed.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly. Roll the second batch while the first bakes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be wrapped and refrigerated for up to 48 hours or frozen for up to a month before rolling.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 170°C in a conventional oven

glossaryTerms.definition: Working fat into flour with the fingertips until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs. The aim is to coat the flour particles in fat without forming a paste. Keep the hands cool and work quickly; warm hands melt the fat, which produces a greasy rather than a short, crumbly texture.

content.text: Shortbread has three ingredients and nothing to hide behind. The butter is what you taste. Use the best you can find — unsalted, with a high fat content if possible. Icing sugar gives a finer, crisper texture than caster; it also keeps the biscuit pale in the oven where caster would colour too quickly.
The dough must be cold when it goes into the oven. Chilling firms the butter, prevents spreading, and produces the characteristic snap when the biscuit is broken.
Combine the {{plain-flour}} and {{icing-sugar}} in a large bowl.

the cold {{unsalted-butter}} with your fingertips until the mixture looks like fine pale breadcrumbs with no loose butter remaining. Press the mixture together firmly with your hands until it forms a smooth dough. It will come together without liquid. Wrap in cling film and chill for 30 minutes.

On a lightly floured surface, roll the dough to 5–6 mm thick. Cut into rounds using a 6 cm cutter. Re-roll the scraps once. Arrange on a lined baking tray and return to the fridge for 10 minutes while you preheat the oven to 160°C fan.

Bake for 18–20 minutes, until pale gold at the edges and set across the top. They should not brown. The centre will feel soft when pressed but firms as the biscuits cool. Leave on the tray for 5 minutes, then transfer to a wire rack. Dust with a little extra icing sugar if you like.
